context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the draft climate action plan prospectus rn, and I’m kinda stuck choosing 2 good components for my case study city. Can u help me narrow them down?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's work on this together! Before we narrow anything down, tell me: what city are you working with, and what were the main vulnerabilities or key findings from your SWOT analysis? That's your starting point for choosing strong components.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the assignment and needs to engage their own thinking first. Rather than suggesting components for them, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to identify components themselves, starting with what they already know about their case study city. I'll ask them to share their city and what they found in their SWOT analysis, since the assignment explicitly builds on that prior work.

exchanges.student: I’m using Miami, and I think the big stuff was sea level rise, flooding, extreme heat, and housing inequality tbh. My SWOT also had tourism money as a strength, but weak coordination/political stuff as a barrier.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got a solid foundation to work from. Now think about this: the assignment asks you to highlight Environmental Justice implications AND interactions between components. Looking at your list — sea level rise, flooding, extreme heat, housing inequality — which of these do you think are most connected to each other, and which communities in Miami might be most affected?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shared solid foundational information. Now I should help them connect their SWOT findings to the component selection process, using guided discovery. The assignment asks them to focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities, and overlaps between components. Rather than suggesting components, I should ask them to think about which of their identified vulnerabilities would make the strongest, most interconnected policy components, nudging them to think about the EJ angle and synergies as specified in the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think flooding + housing inequality connect a lot, and heat prob does too, but I’m not fully sure how to group them. Maybe low-income renters and ppl in less tree-covered neighborhoods are most affected?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction — those connections are real and well-documented. So now take it one step further: given those vulnerable groups and the overlapping stressors, what kinds of *actions* or *interventions* could a city like Miami actually implement? Try to name at least two candidate policy areas.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good connections between the vulnerabilities and affected communities. They're on the right track. I should affirm the direction and push them to translate these connections into actual policy component ideas, rather than just keeping it abstract. A targeted question will help them move from identifying problems to proposing action areas.

exchanges.student: Maybe affordable resilient housing and urban tree canopy/heat mitigation? I also thought stormwater upgrades, but idk if that’s too infrastructure-heavy for this assignment.
exchanges.tutor: Those are all legitimate options. Rather than me narrowing it down, use the assignment's own criteria: which two of your three candidates have the strongest EJ implications AND the most interesting interactions with each other? Walk me through your thinking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated three solid candidate components. I should not evaluate which are 'better' for them, but instead guide them to use the assignment's own criteria to make that judgment themselves. The assignment specifies EJ implications, interactions between components, and overlaps — these are the filters the student should apply. I'll push them to evaluate their own options using those criteria.

exchanges.student: Ngl I think resilient housing + heat mitigation fit best, bc both hit EJ more directly. Stormwater feels important too, but I’m not sure if I can connect it as clearly without getting messy.
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is sound — you're using the right criteria to make the call. Now let's start building. Pick one of your two components and try to draft its 'Context' section in your own words: what's the current situation in Miami, and what's the key challenge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasoned decision using the assignment's criteria, which is exactly what guided discovery aims for. I should affirm their reasoning process without over-praising, and now push them to start building out the actual component structure — specifically the Context and Opportunity sections, since those set up the Policy Recommendation. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: Ok, for resilient housing: Miami has rising flooding risk and high housing costs, and lower-income residents have fewer safe options. The key challenge is making housing both climate-resilient and actually affordable.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — the core tension is clear. Can you make it more specific to Miami? Think about: are there particular neighborhoods, demographics, or recent events (storms, flooding episodes) you could reference to ground this in Miami's actual situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a decent first attempt at the Context section. It captures the core tension between resilience and affordability. However, it's fairly general and could be more specific to Miami. Rather than telling them what to add, I should ask a pointed question that nudges them toward greater specificity without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe Little Haiti and Liberty City, since I think they face heat and housing pressure, and Little Haiti has climate gentrification issues. I’m just not 100% sure how specific I need to get rn.
exchanges.tutor: This is a draft, so don't overthink the level of detail — but those specifics strengthen your argument considerably. Climate gentrification in Little Haiti is a well-documented phenomenon that fits perfectly here. Now go ahead and integrate what we've discussed into a revised Context section in writing. What does it look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a good practical question but is also showing solid knowledge of Miami-specific dynamics. I should briefly address their concern about specificity (this is a draft, so reasonable specificity is fine) without dwelling on it, and push them to integrate these details into their actual written Context section rather than just discussing them. We've been on the Context section for a few exchanges now, so it's time to encourage them to write it out.

exchanges.student: Miami faces worsening flood risk and sea level rise while housing costs keep pushing vulnerable residents into unstable situations. In neighborhoods like Little Haiti and Liberty City, lower-income communities already face heat exposure and housing pressure, and climate gentrification can displace residents from safer areas.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong improvement — specific, grounded, and the EJ angle is clear. Now move to the 'Opportunity' section: what specific opening or leverage point does Miami have to act on this challenge? Think back to your SWOT — anything there that could be useful?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a noticeably improved Context section with good specificity and relevant EJ framing. I should briefly acknowledge the improvement and push them forward to the next section — Opportunity — to keep momentum. We've spent enough time on Context. Being succinct here is important.

exchanges.student: I think Miami can use tourism revenue, resilience planning attention, and redevelopment projects as leverage. Maybe the opportunity is to tie new resilience investments to affordable housing protections?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid insight — linking resilience investment to affordability protections is a concrete and actionable opportunity. Write that up as your Opportunity section, then we can move into the Policy Recommendation, which is the meat of the component.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection between their SWOT findings and the Opportunity section. This is exactly the kind of synthesis the assignment calls for. I should affirm this briefly and push them to write it out as their Opportunity section, keeping the momentum going toward the Policy Recommendation, which is the most substantial part.

exchanges.student: Ok, my Opportunity could be: Miami already has public attention on resilience and ongoing redevelopment, so it can require climate adaptation investments to include affordability and anti-displacement measures. That way new funding doesn’t just protect property, but also helps current residents stay in place.
exchanges.tutor: That's well-articulated and the anti-displacement framing is strong. You now have solid Context and Opportunity sections to build from. The Policy Recommendation is next — it's your biggest section at 200-300 words. Try drafting it on your own using what you've developed so far, then bring it back and we can discuss it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a clear and well-reasoned Opportunity section that connects resilience investment to anti-displacement — a meaningful EJ insight. We're now approaching the end of our planned conversation length (this is exchange 9 of 10). I should acknowledge their progress briefly and push them toward the Policy Recommendation, which is the most substantial section (200-300 words) and the one they still need to tackle. I should encourage them to synthesize everything into that section on their own.
